items.title: Warsh nomination regime change
items.content:
The Warsh nomination changes everything.

"Higher for longer" just became policy, not speculation.

Meanwhile, Treasury is actively draining liquidity via TGA replenishment.

Two headwinds. One direction.

This isn't a dip. It's a regime change.

items.title: BTC -30% liquidity story
items.content:
BTC down 30% in a week.

Not because of crypto news.
Because liquidity is leaving the building.

TGA draining. Hawkish Fed incoming. Risk-off across the board.

This is macro, not micro.

When liquidity contracts, BTC acts like a leveraged Nasdaq bet.

Because that's what it is.

items.title: Liquidity leaving the building meme
items.content:
Top: When the Fed says "higher for longer"
Bottom: Liquidity has left the building

[Elvis leaving building style]

items.title: AI in Procurement - Organizational Challenge
items.content:
Most procurement teams aren't ready for AI.

Not because of technology. Because of structure.

I've seen $50M savings opportunities die in committee because:
→ No clear AI owner
→ IT vs. Procurement turf wars
→ "We've always done it this way"

The winners? They create a small, empowered team. 3-5 people. Direct exec sponsor. Authority to experiment.

AI doesn't fail at procurement.

Organizations fail at change management.

[Link: SCMR article on AI readiness]

items.title: Predictive vs Reactive Supply Chains
items.content:
Every CPO says they want predictive procurement.

But here's what their tech stack actually does:
→ Reactive alerts after the problem
→ Historical dashboards showing what broke
→ Manual escalation paths

Predictive means:
→ Risk signals before disruption
→ Automated scenario modeling
→ Pre-approved contingency actions

The gap isn't AI. It's process redesign.

You can't bolt predictive onto a reactive org.

items.title: Fed doesn't care
items.content: The Fed doesn't care about your bags.
